schools.summary: The law of the trespass offering, most holy: 'likewise this is the law of the trespass offering: it is most holy' — the law of the trespass offering (the offering for trespasses requiring restitution, Leviticus 5:14-6:7), and it being most holy (the trespass offering, the offering for wrongs done, with satisfaction, the most holy, the highest holiness, like the sin offering, eaten only by the priests in the holy place, the figure, the trespass offering, the satisfaction for sin, the figure of Christ's offering, Isaiah 53:10, 'thou shalt make his soul an offering for sin'); the trespass offering (the offering with restitution, the satisfaction for the wrong, Leviticus 5:14-6:7) — the figure (Christ the trespass offering, the satisfaction, Isaiah 53:10); 'it is most holy' (the most holy, the highest holiness, like the sin offering, the figure, the holiness of the offering, the sacredness, the providence, the trespass offering most holy, the figure of Christ's satisfaction) — Henry, Gill, Ellicott.

schools.summary: The killing of the trespass offering and the sprinkling of its blood: 'in the place where they kill the burnt offering shall they kill the trespass offering: and the blood thereof shall he sprinkle round about upon the altar' — the trespass offering killed in the place of the burnt offering, and its blood sprinkled round about on the altar (the place, the same as the burnt offering, the appointed place of sacrifice, Leviticus 1:11, the killing, the death of the victim, the atonement, the blood sprinkled round about, the blood applied to the altar on every side, the atonement, the figure, the death and the blood, the atonement, Christ); the trespass offering killed in the place of the burnt offering (the holy place, Leviticus 1:11) — the figure (the death of the victim, the atonement); 'the blood thereof shall he sprinkle round about upon the altar' (the blood sprinkled round about, the atonement applied on every side, the figure, the blood of atonement, Christ's blood, Hebrews 9:22, the providence, the blood sprinkled, the atonement made) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The offering of the fat of the trespass offering: 'and he shall offer of it all the fat thereof; the rump, and the fat that covereth the inwards' — all the fat offered (the rump or fat tail, and the fat covering the inwards, the fat, the best, richest part, burned to God, Leviticus 3:9-10, the rump, the fat tail of the sheep, the choicest, the inwards, the inner parts, the seat of the affections, the figure, the best given to God, the inward devotion); the fat and the rump (the best parts, burned to God, Leviticus 3:9) — the figure (the best, the inward, given to God); 'the fat that covereth the inwards' (the fat of the inner parts, the seat of the affections, the figure, the inward devotion, the heart given to God, the providence, the fat reserved for God, the best burned) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The taking of the kidneys and the caul: 'and the two kidneys, and the fat that is on them, which is by the flanks, and the caul that is above the liver, with the kidneys, it shall he take away' — the kidneys with their fat and the caul above the liver taken away to be burned (the kidneys, the inner organs, with their fat, Leviticus 3:4, the caul, the membrane over the liver, the figure, the inward parts given to God, the seat of the affections, the inner man offered); the kidneys and their fat (the inward parts, Leviticus 3:4) — the figure (the inward, the seat of feeling, given to God); 'the caul that is above the liver, with the kidneys, it shall he take away' (the caul and kidneys taken away, the inward parts burned, Leviticus 3:4, the figure, the inner man, the affections, given to God, the providence, the inward parts reserved for God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The burning of the fat as a trespass offering: 'and the priest shall burn them upon the altar for an offering made by fire unto the LORD: it is a trespass offering' — the priest burning the fat and inward parts on the altar as an offering made by fire, the trespass offering (the burning, the fat burned to God, the best given, the offering made by fire, the offering consumed, the sweet savour, the figure, the best given to God even in the trespass offering, the inward devotion of Christ, Ephesians 5:2); the fat burned (the best to God, the offering by fire, Leviticus 3:5) — the figure (the sweet savour, the devotion); 'it is a trespass offering' (the trespass offering, the offering for the wrong, the satisfaction, the figure, Christ the trespass offering, the satisfaction, Isaiah 53:10, the providence, the fat burned, the trespass offering complete) — Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: The one law for the sin and trespass offerings, and the priest's portion: 'as the sin offering is, so is the trespass offering: there is one law for them: the priest that maketh atonement therewith shall have it' — the sin and trespass offerings having one law (the same treatment), and the priest who made atonement having the offering as his portion (the one law, the sin and trespass offerings alike, both most holy, both for atonement, the priest's portion, the officiating priest receiving the offering, the maintenance of the ministry, the figure, the offerings for atonement, the priest fed from them); the sin and trespass offerings alike (one law, both most holy) — the likeness (both for atonement, both the priest's portion); 'the priest that maketh atonement therewith shall have it' (the officiating priest's portion, the offering his, the ministry's living, 1 Corinthians 9:13, the figure, the priest fed from the offering, the providence, the priest's portion, the maintenance of the ministry) — JFB, Gill, Wesley.

schools.summary: The baked meal offering as the priest's portion: 'and all the meat offering that is baken in the oven, and all that is dressed in the fryingpan, and in the pan, shall be the priest's that offereth it' — every cooked meal offering (baked in the oven, dressed in the fryingpan or pan) belonging to the priest who offered it (the cooked offerings, the prepared meal offerings, the various ways of cooking, Leviticus 2:4-7, the priest's portion, the officiating priest receiving the cooked offering, the figure, the priest fed from the offering, the ministry maintained); the cooked meal offering the priest's (the officiating priest's portion, Leviticus 2:4) — the provision (the priest fed from the offering); 'and in the pan, shall be the priest's that offereth it' (the various cooked offerings, all the priest's, the providence, the priest's portion, the maintenance of the ministry, 1 Corinthians 9:13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The peace offering of thanksgiving: 'if he offer it for a thanksgiving, then he shall offer with the sacrifice of thanksgiving unleavened cakes mingled with oil, and unleavened wafers anointed with oil, and cakes mingled with oil, of fine flour, fried' — the thank offering — 'if he offer it for a thanksgiving... with the sacrifice of thanksgiving unleavened cakes mingled with oil': one kind of peace offering being the thanksgiving offering, brought with unleavened cakes and wafers mingled with oil (the witnesses noting the significance — the sacrifice of thanksgiving, the peace offering of thankful praise, offered in gratitude for mercies received, the joyful acknowledgment of God's goodness, the lesson — the offering of thanksgiving and praise to God, the thank offering, the response of the grateful heart, 'offer unto God thanksgiving,' Psalm 50:14, 23, 'whoso offereth praise glorifieth me'; Psalm 116:17, 'I will offer to thee the sacrifice of thanksgiving,' the figure — the Christian sacrifice of praise, the fruit of thankful lips, Hebrews 13:15, 'by him therefore let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually, that is, the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name,' the cakes mingled with oil, the offering of devotion mingled with the Spirit's grace, the joyful thanksgiving for God's mercies); the unleavened cakes and wafers mingled and anointed with oil (the offering of fine flour and oil, the purity and the Spirit's grace) — the figure (the unleavened sincerity, the oil of the Spirit, the devotion offered with thanksgiving); the thanksgiving offering — the sacrifice of praise (the providence, the thank offering, the figure of the Christian sacrifice of praise, Hebrews 13:15) — Calvin, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What does the thanksgiving peace offering signify?

sides.position: The thank offering of grateful praise for God's mercies (Psalm 50:14, 23; 116:17) - a figure of the Christian sacrifice of praise, the fruit of thankful lips offered continually through Christ (Hebrews 13:15), the cakes mingled with oil the devotion mingled with the Spirit's grace

schools.summary: The thanksgiving flesh eaten the same day: 'and the flesh of the sacrifice of his peace offerings for thanksgiving shall be eaten the same day that it is offered; he shall not leave any of it until the morning' — the flesh of the thanksgiving peace offering eaten the same day, none left till morning (the same day, the flesh eaten promptly, the festal meal that day, not kept, the none left, nothing kept overnight, the offering wholly used that day, the figure, the prompt enjoyment of the fellowship, the freshness of the thanksgiving, perhaps the daily, present enjoyment of communion, not stale or delayed); the flesh eaten the same day (the festal meal, the prompt enjoyment, not kept) — the freshness (the communion enjoyed promptly); 'he shall not leave any of it until the morning' (nothing kept overnight, the offering wholly used that day, the freshness, the figure, the present, fresh enjoyment of fellowship, the danger of stale or delayed devotion, the providence, the thanksgiving eaten the same day, the prompt communion) — Clarke, Gill.

schools.summary: The vow and voluntary peace offerings: 'but if the sacrifice of his offering be a vow, or a voluntary offering, it shall be eaten the same day that he offereth his sacrifice: and on the morrow also the remainder of it shall be eaten' — besides the thanksgiving offering, the vow offering (in fulfillment of a vow) and the voluntary (freewill) offering, which might be eaten the same day and the next (the vow offering, the offering paying a vow, Psalm 50:14; 66:13-14, the voluntary offering, the freewill gift, not required, the spontaneous devotion, the two days, the vow and freewill offerings eaten over two days, more latitude than the thanksgiving); the vow offering (the paying of a vow, the offering promised to God, Ecclesiastes 5:4-5, 'when thou vowest a vow... defer not to pay it') — the figure (the keeping of vows to God, the payment of what is promised); 'or a voluntary offering... on the morrow also the remainder of it shall be eaten' (the voluntary, freewill, offering, the spontaneous devotion, the cheerful gift, the two days, the latitude of eating, the figure, the freewill devotion, the cheerful offering, the providence, the vow and freewill offerings, the figure of vows kept and free devotion) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

schools.summary: The offering eaten too late rejected as an abomination: 'and if any of the flesh of the sacrifice of his peace offerings be eaten at all on the third day, it shall not be accepted, neither shall it be imputed unto him that offereth it: it shall be an abomination, and the soul that eateth of it shall bear his iniquity' — the abomination — 'if any of the flesh... be eaten on the third day, it shall not be accepted, neither shall it be imputed unto him... it shall be an abomination': the offering eaten beyond the appointed time not accepted, not credited to the offerer, but an abomination, and the eater bearing his iniquity (the witnesses noting the significance — the offering rejected if not used in God's appointed way, the sacrifice, however rightly begun, made void by the disobedience in the eating, the corrupted, stale flesh an abomination, the lesson — the worship must be according to God's appointment to be accepted, the offering not accepted nor imputed if profaned by disobedience, God regards not only the offering but the obedience in its use, 'to obey is better than sacrifice,' 1 Samuel 15:22, the figure — the worship rejected when offered in man's way and not God's, the will-worship that profanes, the sacrifice not accepted, the offerer bearing his iniquity, the corruption that makes the holy an abomination); the offering not accepted nor imputed (the sacrifice made void, the offerer gaining nothing) — the lesson (the worship profaned not accepted, the obedience required, the figure, the rejected offering, the will-worship); 'it shall be an abomination, and the soul that eateth of it shall bear his iniquity' (the corrupted offering an abomination, the eater bearing his guilt, the providence, the offering rejected, the disobedience bearing iniquity) — Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Why is the offering eaten too late rejected and an abomination?

sides.position: The sacrifice, however rightly begun, made void and an abomination by disobedience in its use (not accepted nor imputed) - the worship rejected when offered in man's way and not God's ('to obey is better than sacrifice,' 1 Samuel 15:22), the will-worship that profanes the holy, the eater bearing his iniquity

schools.summary: The unclean flesh not eaten and the clean partaking: 'and the flesh that toucheth any unclean thing shall not be eaten; it shall be burnt with fire: and as for the flesh, all that be clean shall eat thereof' — flesh of the peace offering that touched any unclean thing not eaten but burned, and only the clean might eat of the holy flesh (the unclean flesh, defiled by contact, not to be eaten, burned, the clean eating, only the ceremonially clean partaking of the holy flesh, the figure, the holy food not to be defiled, only the clean partaking, the holiness required of those who commune, the discerning of the holy); the defiled flesh not eaten but burned (the holy not to be profaned) — the holiness (the holy food guarded from uncleanness); 'all that be clean shall eat thereof' (only the clean eating the holy flesh, the ceremonial purity required, the figure, the holiness required of those who commune, the clean alone partaking, the worthy partaking, 1 Corinthians 11:27-29, the providence, the holy food for the clean, the holiness guarded) — JFB, Gill, Calvin.

schools.summary: The unclean communicant cut off: 'but the soul that eateth of the flesh of the sacrifice of peace offerings, that pertain unto the LORD, having his uncleanness upon him, even that soul shall be cut off from his people' — the cutting off — 'the soul that eateth of the flesh of the sacrifice of peace offerings, that pertain unto the LORD, having his uncleanness upon him, even that soul shall be cut off from his people': whoever ate of the holy peace offering while ceremonially unclean to be cut off (the witnesses noting the significance — the holiness required of those who eat at God's table, the unclean not to partake of the holy, on pain of being cut off, the severity of profaning the holy communion, the lesson — the reverence and purity required in approaching God's holy things, the holy food not to be eaten in uncleanness, the profaning of the sacred punished by excision, the figure — the warning against unworthy partaking of the Lord's table, the believer to examine himself, 'let a man examine himself, and so let him eat... for he that eateth and drinketh unworthily, eateth and drinketh damnation to himself, not discerning the Lord's body,' 1 Corinthians 11:28-29, the holiness required of those who commune, the discerning of the holy, the danger of profaning the sacred, the cutting off, the excision of the profane, the severity of God's holiness); the offerings as the LORD's (the holy things, belonging to God, not to be profaned) — the holiness (the sacred not to be defiled by the unclean); 'even that soul shall be cut off from his people' (the excision, the profane cut off, the severity, the figure, the warning against unworthy communion, 1 Corinthians 11:27-29, the providence, the holiness of the table guarded, the unclean cut off) — Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What does the cutting off of the unclean communicant teach?

sides.position: The holiness required of those who eat at God's table - the unclean not to partake of the holy on pain of excision, the reverence and purity in approaching God's holy things; a figure of the warning against unworthy partaking of the Lord's table, the self-examination and discerning of the Lord's body required (1 Corinthians 11:28-29)

schools.summary: The prohibition of eating the fat: 'speak unto the children of Israel, saying, Ye shall eat no manner of fat, of ox, or of sheep, or of goat' — Israel forbidden to eat the fat (the inner fat, the suet) of the sacrificial animals (ox, sheep, goat, the fat, the inner fat, the choicest, reserved for God on the altar, Leviticus 3:16-17, the prohibition, the fat not to be eaten, belonging to God, the reason, the fat the best, given to God, the figure of the best reserved for him); the fat reserved for God (the best, the choicest, burned on the altar, not eaten, Leviticus 3:16) — the figure (the best given to God, the inward devotion reserved for him, the lesson, God claiming the best); 'of ox, or of sheep, or of goat' (the fat of the sacrificial animals, those offered, the inner fat reserved, the providence, the fat of the offerings reserved to God, the perpetual statute, vv.25, the figure, the best of the offering for God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

themes: Speak unto the children of Israel, saying, Ye shall eat no manner of fat, of ox, or of sheep, or of goat

schools.summary: The fat of the dead used but not eaten: 'and the fat of the beast that dieth of itself, and the fat of that which is torn with beasts, may be used in any other use: but ye shall in no wise eat of it' — the fat of an animal that died of itself or was torn by beasts (not sacrificial) might be put to other uses (not as food), but was not to be eaten (the fat of the dead, not from a sacrifice, the animal died or was torn, the other use, the fat used for other purposes, not eaten, the prohibition, the fat in no wise eaten, the reverence for the fat, the figure, the fat reserved, not eaten, even of the non-sacrificial); the fat of the dead used for other purposes (not as food, the latitude for non-eating uses) — the use (the fat not wasted, but not eaten); 'but ye shall in no wise eat of it' (the fat in no wise eaten, the strict prohibition, the reverence for the fat, the figure, the fat reserved, not for food, the providence, the prohibition of eating fat, even of the dead) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the fat of the beast that dieth of itself, and the fat of that which is torn with beasts, may be used in any other use: but ye shall in no wise eat of it

schools.summary: The cutting off for eating the fat of the offering: 'for whosoever eateth the fat of the beast, of which men offer an offering made by fire unto the LORD, even the soul that eateth it shall be cut off from his people' — whoever ate the fat of an animal of the kind offered to the LORD to be cut off (the fat of the offering, the fat of the sacrificial animals, reserved for God, the cutting off, the excision, the severity of taking what is God's, the figure, the fat God's portion, not to be taken, the reverence for what is reserved to God); the fat of the sacrificial beast (reserved for God, not eaten) — the reservation (the fat God's, not to be taken); 'even the soul that eateth it shall be cut off from his people' (the excision, the one taking God's portion cut off, the figure, the reverence for what is God's, the severity of robbing God, the providence, the fat reserved, the eater cut off) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The prohibition of eating blood: 'moreover ye shall eat no manner of blood, whether it be of fowl or of beast, in any of your dwellings' — the command — 'ye shall eat no manner of blood, whether it be of fowl or of beast, in any of your dwellings': Israel forbidden to eat any blood, of bird or beast, in any of their homes (the witnesses noting the significance — the prohibition of blood, the blood not to be eaten, a perpetual and universal statute, the lesson — the reason given in Leviticus 17:11, 'the life of the flesh is in the blood: and I have given it to you upon the altar to make an atonement for your souls: for it is the blood that maketh an atonement,' the blood the life, reserved for God and for atonement, too sacred to be eaten, the blood the seat of life, the means of atonement, not to be treated as common food, the antiquity of the law, the prohibition of blood given to Noah, Genesis 9:4, before the law, and its weight in the New Testament, the apostolic decree, 'abstain... from blood,' Acts 15:20, 29, the figure — the blood the means of atonement, the precious blood of Christ, the life poured out for sin, too sacred to be profaned, 1 Peter 1:18-19; Hebrews 9:22); the blood of any creature, in any dwelling (the universal, perpetual prohibition) — the reverence (the blood reserved, not eaten, the figure, the sacredness of the blood, the life, the atonement); no blood eaten — the blood reserved for atonement (the providence, the blood the life and the means of atonement, reserved to God) — Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Why is the eating of blood forbidden?

sides.position: The blood is the life, reserved for God and given for atonement (Leviticus 17:11) - too sacred to be eaten as common food, a law from Noah onward (Genesis 9:4) and upheld in the apostolic decree (Acts 15:20, 29), a figure of the precious blood of Christ poured out for sin (1 Peter 1:18-19; Hebrews 9:22)

themes: Moreover ye shall eat no manner of blood, whether it be of fowl or of beast, in any of your dwellings
the prohibition of eating blood (the crux of the blood reserved for atonement)

schools.summary: The cutting off for eating blood: 'whatsoever soul it be that eateth any manner of blood, even that soul shall be cut off from his people' — whoever ate any blood to be cut off (the eating blood, the breach of the prohibition, vv.26, the cutting off, the excision, the severity of profaning the blood, the figure, the sacredness of the blood, the life and the atonement, the severity of treating it as common, Hebrews 10:29); the one eating any blood (the breach of the perpetual statute, vv.26) — the breach (the profaning of the blood); 'even that soul shall be cut off from his people' (the excision, the one profaning the blood cut off, the figure, the reverence for the blood, the severity of treating the sacred as common, the providence, the blood reserved, the eater cut off) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: Whatsoever soul it be that eateth any manner of blood, even that soul shall be cut off from his people

schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning the priests' portion of the peace offerings: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses, now to give the law of the priests' portion of the peace offerings (the wave breast and heave shoulder, vv.29-36, the speaking, God's command, the new section, the occasion, after the prohibition of fat and blood, now the priests' portion of the peace offerings, a fresh word from the LORD opening the law of the priests' portion, marking the care with which God provided for those who minister at his altar); the LORD speaking (the priests' portion of the peace offerings) — the new law (the wave breast and heave shoulder for the priests); the LORD spake unto Moses — the priests' portion introduced (the providence, God providing for the priests from the peace offerings) — Henry, Gill, Ellicott.

schools.summary: The offerer bringing his own peace offering: 'speak unto the children of Israel, saying, He that offereth the sacrifice of his peace offerings unto the LORD shall bring his oblation unto the LORD of the sacrifice of his peace offerings' — the one who offered a peace offering himself to bring his oblation to the LORD (the offerer bringing it, the worshipper bringing his own offering, the personal devotion, the his own, the offering brought by the offerer himself, the personal act of worship, the figure, the personal bringing of one's offering, the worshipper's own devotion, not by proxy); the offerer bringing his own peace offering (the personal devotion, the worshipper's own act) — the personal worship (the offering brought by the offerer); 'shall bring his oblation unto the LORD' (the offering brought to the LORD, the personal act, the devotion, the figure, the worshipper's own bringing, the personal offering, Romans 12:1, the providence, the offerer bringing his offering, the wave breast and heave shoulder to follow, vv.30) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

schools.summary: The offerer's own hands bringing the fat and the breast for the wave offering: 'his own hands shall bring the offerings of the LORD made by fire, the fat with the breast, it shall he bring, that the breast may be waved for a wave offering before the LORD' — the offerer's own hands bringing the fat (for the altar) and the breast (to be waved, the his own hands, the personal act, the worshipper's own hands bringing the offering, the fat, for the altar, God's portion, the breast waved, the wave offering, moved to and fro before the LORD, dedicated to God and given to the priests, vv.31, the figure, the personal devotion, the wave offering presented to God); the offerer's own hands bringing the offering (the personal devotion, vv.29) — the personal worship (the worshipper's own hands); 'that the breast may be waved for a wave offering before the LORD' (the breast waved, the wave offering, dedicated to God by the waving, then given to the priests, vv.31, the figure, the offering presented to God, dedicated, then shared, the providence, the wave breast, the priests' portion, vv.31) — Clarke, Gill, Wesley.

themes: His own hands shall bring the offerings of the LORD made by fire, the fat with the breast, it shall he bring, that the breast may be waved for a wave offering before the LORD

schools.summary: The fat burned and the breast given to the priests: 'and the priest shall burn the fat upon the altar: but the breast shall be Aaron's and his sons'' — the fat burned on the altar (God's portion), and the breast (the waved breast) going to the priests (the fat burned, God's portion, the best, the breast to the priests, the wave breast, the priests' portion, Aaron's and his sons', the figure, God's portion and the priests' portion, the fellowship of the peace offering shared between God, the priests, and the offerer); the fat burned to God (the best, God's portion, Leviticus 3:16) — the figure (the best given to God); 'but the breast shall be Aaron's and his sons'' (the wave breast to the priests, the priests' portion, the breast, the figure, the priests fed from the offering, the breast, perhaps the figure of the love and affection of the offering shared, the providence, the breast the priests' portion, the fellowship shared) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And the priest shall burn the fat upon the altar: but the breast shall be Aaron's and his sons'

schools.summary: The right shoulder given to the priest as a heave offering: 'and the right shoulder shall ye give unto the priest for an heave offering of the sacrifices of your peace offerings' — the right shoulder (the choicest, strongest part) given to the priest as a heave offering (the right shoulder, the best part, the strength, heaved up to God and given to the priest, the heave offering, lifted up, dedicated to God, then given to the priest, the figure, the strength and the best given to God and his minister, the wave breast, the love, and the heave shoulder, the strength, the priests' portion); the right shoulder to the priest (the best part, the heave offering, the priests' portion) — the figure (the strength given, the priest's portion); 'for an heave offering of the sacrifices of your peace offerings' (the shoulder heaved, dedicated to God, given to the priest, the figure, the strength of the offering, the best for God and his minister, the providence, the heave shoulder, the priests' portion, vv.33-34) — Clarke, Gill.

schools.summary: The right shoulder given to the officiating priest: 'he among the sons of Aaron, that offereth the blood of the peace offerings, and the fat, shall have the right shoulder for his part' — the priest who offered the blood and fat (the officiating priest) receiving the right shoulder as his particular portion (the officiating priest, the one who did the service, the blood and fat, the right shoulder, his particular portion, the reward of his service, the difference, the breast shared by all the priests, vv.31, the shoulder to the officiating priest, the figure, the priest rewarded according to his service); the priest who offered the blood and fat (the officiating one, vv.31) — the service (the one doing the work); 'shall have the right shoulder for his part' (the right shoulder his particular portion, the reward of his service, the figure, the priest rewarded according to his labor, 1 Corinthians 9:13-14, the providence, the officiating priest's portion) — Gill.

schools.summary: The wave breast and heave shoulder given to the priests for ever: 'for the wave breast and the heave shoulder have I taken of the children of Israel from off the sacrifices of their peace offerings, and have given them unto Aaron the priest and unto his sons by a statute for ever from among the children of Israel' — the perpetual due — 'the wave breast and the heave shoulder have I taken of the children of Israel... and have given them unto Aaron the priest and unto his sons by a statute for ever': God taking the wave breast and heave shoulder from the peace offerings and giving them to the priests as a perpetual due (the witnesses noting the significance — the priests' perpetual portion, the breast and shoulder, God's gift to his ministers, a statute for ever, the lesson — God's provision for those who minister at his altar, the priests fed from the offerings, the ministry sustained by the people's worship, 'the LORD hath ordained that they which preach the gospel should live of the gospel,' 1 Corinthians 9:13-14, 'they which wait at the altar are partakers with the altar,' the wave breast and heave shoulder, the breast, perhaps the figure of the love and affection, and the shoulder, the strength — the love and strength of the offering, the best given to the priests, the figure — the provision for God's servants, and the fellowship of the peace offering shared between God, the fat, the priests, the breast and shoulder, and the offerer, the rest, the communion of the redeemed with God and his ministers); God's gift to the priests for ever (the perpetual portion, the provision for the ministry) — the provision (the ministry sustained, the priests fed from the altar); the wave breast and heave shoulder — the priests' portion (the providence, God's provision for his ministers, the fellowship shared) — Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the gift of the breast and shoulder to the priests teach?

sides.position: God's provision for those who minister at his altar - the priests fed from the offerings, the ministry sustained by the people's worship (1 Corinthians 9:13-14); the breast and shoulder (love and strength) the best given to the priests, the fellowship of the peace offering shared between God, the priests, and the offerer

themes: For the wave breast and the heave shoulder have I taken of the children of Israel... and have given them unto Aaron the priest and unto his sons by a statute for ever

schools.summary: The priests' portion appointed from the day of their anointing: 'this is the portion of the anointing of Aaron, and of the anointing of his sons, out of the offerings of the LORD made by fire, in the day when he presented them to minister unto the LORD in the priest's office' — this (the breast and shoulder, vv.34) being the portion appointed to Aaron and his sons from the day of their anointing and presentation to the priesthood (the portion of the anointing, the priests' due, appointed at their consecration, the from the offerings, the priests fed from the offerings, the day of their presentation, from the beginning of their ministry, the figure, the provision appointed with the office, the ministry sustained from the offerings); the priests' portion (appointed at their anointing, the breast and shoulder) — the provision (the priests' due from their consecration); 'in the day when he presented them to minister unto the LORD in the priest's office' (the portion from the start of the ministry, the provision appointed with the office, the figure, the ministry sustained from the offerings, 1 Corinthians 9:13-14, the providence, the priests' portion, the provision with the office) — Henry, Gill, Wesley.

schools.summary: The priests' portion a perpetual statute: 'which the LORD commanded to be given them of the children of Israel, in the day that he anointed them, by a statute for ever throughout their generations' — the LORD commanding the portion to be given to the priests from the people, from the day of their anointing, as a perpetual statute throughout their generations (the commanded, God's command, the portion appointed, the by a statute for ever, the perpetual due, throughout the generations, the figure, the perpetual provision for the ministry, God's standing ordinance for his servants, 1 Corinthians 9:13-14); the LORD commanding the portion to the priests (the people providing for the ministry) — the provision (the people sustaining the priests by God's command); 'by a statute for ever throughout their generations' (the perpetual statute, the portion a standing ordinance, the figure, the perpetual provision for the ministry, God's standing order, the providence, the priests' portion perpetual, the ministry sustained) — Clarke, Gill.

schools.summary: The summary of the laws of the offerings: 'this is the law of the burnt offering, of the meat offering, and of the sin offering, and of the trespass offering, and of the consecrations, and of the sacrifice of the peace offerings' — this summing up the laws of all the offerings (burnt, meal, sin, trespass, consecration, peace, the summary, the gathering of all the offerings, chs. 1-7, the offerings, the whole system of sacrifice, each with its meaning, the figure, the offerings together, the manifold aspects of the one sacrifice of Christ — the burnt offering, his whole devotion, the meal offering, his perfect life, the sin and trespass offerings, his atonement, the peace offering, the fellowship he won, the consecration, his self-dedication); the offerings summed up (the whole system, chs. 1-7) — the figure (the manifold offerings, the many aspects of the one sacrifice of Christ, fulfilled in him, Hebrews 10:1-14); 'and of the consecrations, and of the sacrifice of the peace offerings' (the consecration, the self-dedication, and the peace offering, the fellowship, the figure, Christ's self-dedication and the peace and fellowship he won, Colossians 1:20, the providence, the offerings summed up, the figure of Christ's one sacrifice in its manifold aspects) — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott, Wesley.

schools.summary: The laws given at Sinai: 'which the LORD commanded Moses in mount Sinai, in the day that he commanded the children of Israel to offer their oblations unto the LORD, in the wilderness of Sinai' — the laws of the offerings commanded by the LORD to Moses at mount Sinai, when he commanded Israel to offer their oblations, in the wilderness of Sinai (the at Sinai, the laws given at the holy mount, the place of the covenant, the the day he commanded, the time of the giving, when the offerings were instituted, the divine authority, the laws from God, at Sinai, not man's invention, the figure, the offerings divinely appointed, the worship ordered by God); the laws given at Sinai (the holy mount, the place of the covenant) — the authority (the laws from God at Sinai); 'in the day that he commanded the children of Israel to offer their oblations unto the LORD, in the wilderness of Sinai' (the time of the institution, the offerings appointed, in the wilderness, the figure, the worship divinely ordered, the offerings appointed by God, the providence, the laws of the offerings given at Sinai, the close of the section) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.
